responseMessageContents.text:
The environment variables aren't showing up in the sandbox even though the user said they were configured. I checked with printenv and got nothing, which is the same issue that happened before with RENDER_API_KEY. Looking at the setup, it seems private environment variables might be server-only and not injected into the bash sandbox, even though AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY and RENDER_API_KEY are registered in the project.

I should check if the sandbox needs a restart to pick up new variables, or look for them in .env files, /proc/1/environ, or try using the work-agent CLI to fetch them directly.

responseMessageContents.text:
The private environment variables the user set through the widget aren't making it into the bash sandbox, even though they're defined at the project level in the system prompt. They might be restricted to deployed backends like Cloudflare functions rather than the agent's shell environment. I'm trying to understand why RENDER_API_KEY and AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY exist in the project config but aren't accessible here.

I think the solution is to deploy a small Cloudflare Worker function that can access these private environment variables server-side — it would validate the AI gateway key and use the Render API key to update the service's environment variables and trigger a deployment, keeping the keys secure and never exposing them. Let me check the functions folder structure to see how to set this up.

responseMessageContents.text:
The private environment variables aren't accessible in the sandbox shell, but they're available in the deployed Cloudflare Worker environment. I'm planning to update the functions to add two new actions: one that checks and reports which API keys are present, and another that tests the AI Gateway key against Vercel's AI Gateway with a small generation request.

I also need to set up a Render deployment action that uses the Render API key to update the service environment variables and trigger a deploy, plus a status polling action. After deploying the worker via the CLI, I'll test the endpoints with curl and wait for the Render deployment to go live before running an end-to-end chat test.

I need to figure out the worker URL from the deploy output, add the AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY to the Env interface, and hardcode the Render service ID as a fallback. For the Vercel AI Gateway test, I'm checking whether to use the v1 or v3 endpoint—the gateway supports OpenAI-compatible requests, so I can hit the chat completions endpoint with Bearer authentication.

Adding the worker actions to functions/index.ts...I'll use the project's Cloudflare deploy-bridge worker to handle the key operations securely, since it has access to the private environment variables where the keys live.
Now I'm calling the worker's endpoints in sequence—checking the environment, testing the gateway, setting the AI key, monitoring the deployment status, and running the full end-to-end test.
The worker is deployed. Now checking the keys server-side:

Looking at the env-check output, the aiGatewayKey is 72 characters long with a mask starting with "Verc" — which suggests the user may have pasted extra text like "Vercel key: vck_..." instead of just the key itself, since actual Vercel AI Gateway keys should start with "vck_".

The renderApiKey is only 10 characters, which is way too short — Render keys are typically around 34-36 characters total (rnd_ prefix plus ~30 alphanumeric chars), so this one is truncated or incomplete.

I'll update the worker to sanitize these keys server-side by extracting the vck_ token with regex and trimming whitespace, plus add a render-test action that validates the Render key with a GET /services call. Then I'll redeploy and run the full test suite to check if the gateway key works and whether the Render key is salvageable.The keys arrived corrupted again, so I'll add server-side sanitization and validation checks to fix this.

responseMessageContents.text:
Both authentication keys tested server-side are failing — the Gateway key cleaned to 60 characters is returning a 401 error from Vercel, suggesting either the key itself is invalid or revoked, or I'm extracting an incomplete token. The raw length of 72 characters with a "Verc" prefix makes me wonder if the user accidentally pasted descriptive text along with the actual key, and since vck_ keys should be alphanumeric, there might be unexpected characters mixed in that are breaking the validation.

The Render key is even worse — just 10 characters total when real ones should be around 34, so it's clearly truncated. I need to ask the user to re-paste both keys with precise instructions: the full key only, no extra words or descriptions. I'll use requestEnvs again to validate them properly.Both keys failed authentication—the Vercel key appears invalid or incomplete (60 chars instead of the full length), and the Render key was cut off at just 10 characters. Please re-paste both keys in full, copying them completely without any extra text.

envs.description: FULL Vercel AI Gateway key — create a fresh one (AI Gateway → API Keys → Create Key) and paste the entire value, nothing else. Starts with vck_

envs.name: RENDER_API_KEY
envs.description: FULL Render API key — the last paste was cut to 10 characters. Paste the entire value (about 35 chars). Starts with rnd_
